Deploying your App - Using Functions to Streamline Your Code - Shiny Modules

Learning outcomes

These are the skills you’re supposed to walk away with:

  • Create strategies for analyzing text.
    You’re not just running code—you’re learning how to think about text data:
    • What questions to ask (e.g., “Which words are most common?”, “How does language differ between authors?”).
    • How to structure your workflow (cleaning, tokenizing, analyzing, visualizing).
  • Manipulate and analyze text data using {tidytext}.
    {tidytext} is an R package that turns text into “tidy” data: one token (usually a word) per row. That makes it easy to use with the rest of the tidyverse (dplyr, ggplot2, etc.). You’ll learn to:
    • Break text into words or n-grams.
    • Join with other data (like sentiment lexicons).
    • Summarize and visualize results.
  • Frequency analysis.
    This means counting how often words appear:
    • Which words are most common in a document or set of documents?
    • How does word frequency differ across authors, books, time periods, or categories?
    • Often the first step in understanding a text corpus.
  • Relationships among words.
    This is about how words appear together:
    • N-grams (e.g., “New York”, “data science”) to capture phrases.
    • Co-occurrence or correlation between words (which words tend to show up in the same context).
    • This helps reveal themes, topics, or stylistic patterns.
  • Sentiment analysis.
    You’ll learn to:
    • Use sentiment lexicons (lists of words labeled as positive, negative, anger, joy, etc.).
    • Join your text data with these lexicons.
    • Compute sentiment scores for documents, chapters, time periods, or categories.
    • Interpret how “emotional tone” changes across a text or between groups.
  • Build word cloud plots.
    Word clouds visualize word frequency by font size:
    • More frequent words appear larger.
    • It’s a quick, visual summary of what dominates the text.
    • You’ll likely use the {wordcloud} package for this.

References and packages

The references list is basically your toolkit and background reading:

  • Text Mining with R (Silge & Robinson, 2025).
    This is the core book that your material is drawing from. It teaches text mining using tidy data principles and {tidytext}.

  • {tidytext} package (Silge & Robinson, 2016).
    The main R package for tidy text mining:

    • Functions like unnest_tokens() to turn text into tokens.
    • Built-in sentiment lexicons.
    • Tools for tf-idf, n-grams, etc.

  • {stringi} package (Gagolewski, 2026).
    A powerful string manipulation package:

    • Handling Unicode, pattern matching, text cleaning.
    • Often used behind the scenes or for more advanced text wrangling.

  • {janeaustenr} package (Silge, 2023).
    Provides the full text of Jane Austen’s novels:

    • Commonly used as example data for text mining.
    • Great for practicing on real literary text.

  • {gutenbergr} package (Robinson & Johnston, 2023).
    Interface to Project Gutenberg:

    • Lets you download public domain books easily.
    • Useful for building your own text corpora.

  • {wordcloud} package (Fellows, 2018).
    Used to create word cloud visualizations.

  • Other reference: {scales} (Wickham & Seidel, 2022).
    Helps with formatting axes, labels, and scales in plots—useful when visualizing text analysis results.

What “Text Analysis with {tidytext}” means

The section “15.2 Text Analysis with {tidytext}” is telling you:

  • Source of material.
    Much of what you’ll see is adapted from Text Mining with R by Silge and Robinson. So you’re learning a well-established, widely used approach.

  • Definition of text mining.
    > “Text Mining can be considered as a process for extracting insights from text.”
    That’s the core idea: you’re turning unstructured text (books, tweets, reviews, transcripts) into structured information you can analyze.

  • Historical context.

    • Computer-based text mining dates back to:
      • The 1950s with automated translation.
      • The 1940s if you include code-breaking (e.g., WWII cryptanalysis).
    • This shows that using computers to work with language is not new—what’s new is the scale and sophistication.

  • CRAN Task View: NLP.
    There’s a curated list on CRAN of R packages for NLP:

    • Over 50 packages for gathering, organizing, modeling, and analyzing text.
    • {tidytext} is one piece of a larger ecosystem.

How this fits into Natural Language Processing (NLP)

The text then zooms out: text mining is part of the broader field of NLP. It lists several major application areas:

  • Machine translation.
    • Automatically translating text from one language to another (e.g., English → Spanish) without human intervention.
  • Speech recognition.
    • Systems like Alexa, Google Assistant, Siri:
      • Convert spoken language to text.
      • Then interpret that text to figure out what you’re asking.
  • Sentiment analysis.
    • Also called opinion mining or emotion AI.
    • Used to detect attitudes (positive/negative), emotions, or opinions in text:
      • Product reviews, tweets, survey responses, etc.
  • Question answering.
    • Again, Alexa/Google/Siri:
      • Take a natural language question.
      • Retrieve or generate an answer in a way you can understand.
  • Automatic summarization.
    • Reducing long documents into shorter summaries:
      • Extractive (pulling key sentences).
      • Abstractive (generating new text that captures the main ideas).
  • Chatbots.
    • Often combine:
      • Speech recognition (or text input),
      • Question answering, and
      • Some memory/context within a specific domain (e.g., customer support, banking, tech help).
  • Market intelligence.
    • Automated analysis of:
      • Searches, posts, tweets, reviews, etc.
    • Used to understand trends, brand perception, customer needs.
  • Text classification.
    • Automatically assigning categories or tags to text:
      • Spam vs. not spam.
      • Topic labels (sports, politics, tech).
      • Relevance of reference material.
  • Character recognition.
    • Optical Character Recognition (OCR):
      • Turning images of text (scans, photos) into machine-readable text.
  • Spelling and grammar checking.
    • Tools that detect typos, grammar errors, and suggest corrections.

The key message: text analysis/NLP is foundational for generative AI.
Generative AI (like large language models) relies on: - Massive amounts of text data. - Techniques from NLP (tokenization, embeddings, language modeling). - The same underlying idea: representing language in a way computers can work with.

How all of this ties together for you

Putting it all together, this intro is saying:

  1. You’re learning a practical toolkit:
    • {tidytext}, {stringi}, {wordcloud}, {janeaustenr}, {gutenbergr}, etc.
    • You’ll use them to clean, structure, and analyze text.
  2. You’ll practice core text mining tasks:
    • Frequency analysis.
    • Relationships among words.
    • Sentiment analysis.
    • Visualizations like word clouds.
  3. You’re stepping into the broader world of NLP:
    • The same ideas you use on small corpora in R scale up to real-world systems:
      • Search engines, chatbots, translation, generative AI.

Ways to organize text for analysis

You’re being shown several common data structures for text. They all represent the same underlying thing—language—but in very different shapes.

1. Strings

  • What it is:
    Plain character data—just text stored as:
    • Atomic vectors (e.g., a character vector where each element is a document or a line), or
    • Columns in a data frame/tibble.
  • Pros:
    • Simple, natural way to store text.
    • Good for reading, printing, and basic manipulation.
  • Cons:
    • Hard to analyze directly.
    • You usually need to transform strings into something more structured (tokens, counts, etc.) before doing serious analysis.

2. Corpus

  • What it is:
    A corpus is a collection (library) of documents, where each document:
    • Contains the text (as a string).
    • Has metadata: author, title, date, source, language, etc.
  • Why it matters:
    • Many NLP frameworks (like tm, quanteda) use corpus objects.
    • Metadata is crucial for grouping, comparing, and modeling (e.g., sentiment by author, topic by year).

3. Document–Term Matrix (DTM)

  • What it is:
    A matrix where:
    • Rows = documents
    • Columns = terms (words or tokens)
    • Entries = some numeric value for how strongly that term appears in that document.
  • Common values in the cells:
    • Raw counts: how many times the word appears in the document.
    • TF–IDF:
      • Term Frequency (TF): how often a term appears in a document.
      • Inverse Document Frequency (IDF): downweights terms that appear in many documents (like “the”, “and”).
      • TF–IDF highlights words that are important for a specific document.
  • Sparse matrix:
    • Most documents use only a small fraction of all possible words.
    • So most entries in the DTM are 0.
    • To save memory and speed up computation, DTMs are usually stored as sparse matrices (only non-zero entries are stored).
  • Term–Document Matrix (TDM):
    • Just the transpose of the DTM:
      • Rows = terms, columns = documents.
    • Same information, different orientation.

Tidy text format (TTF)

Now we get to the main idea: Tidy Text Format.

What TTF is

  • Goal:
    Organize text so you can use the tidyverse (dplyr, ggplot2, etc.) easily.

  • Definition:
    A tidy text table (tibble/data frame) where:

    • Each row = one token.
    • Other columns = document ID, metadata, counts, etc.

  • Why this is powerful:

    • You can use familiar verbs:
      • filter() to subset tokens.
      • count() to get frequencies.
      • group_by() to compare across documents, authors, time.
      • inner_join() to add sentiment labels or other lexicons.
    • It’s consistent with the broader tidy data philosophy:
      • Each variable is a column.
      • Each observation is a row.

What is a token?

  • Token:
    A “meaningful unit of text” that you decide based on your analysis.

  • Examples of tokens:

    • Word (most common).
    • N-gram: sequence of ( n ) words (e.g., bigrams like “New York”, trigrams like “machine learning model”).
    • Sentence.
    • Paragraph.
    • Larger units: chapters, sections, even whole documents.

  • Key idea:
    You choose the token level that matches your question:

    • If you care about vocabulary: words.
    • If you care about phrases: n-grams.
    • If you care about narrative structure: sentences or paragraphs.

Bag of words vs. more advanced representations

Bag of words

  • What it is:
    You treat a document as a “bag” of tokens:
    • You ignore order.
    • You ignore syntax (grammar, sentence structure).
    • You only care about which tokens appear and how often.
  • In practice:
    • DTMs and TTF word counts are classic bag-of-words representations.
    • Great for:
      • Frequency analysis.
      • Basic classification.
      • Simple sentiment analysis.
  • Limitation:
    • Loses context:
      • “not good” and “good” both contain “good”.
      • Word order, negation, sarcasm, and subtle meaning are not captured.

Neural word embeddings

  • Why they exist:
    To move beyond bag-of-words and capture context and semantic relationships.

  • What they do:

    • Represent each word as a vector of numbers (an embedding).
    • These vectors are learned from large corpora so that:
      • Words used in similar contexts have similar vectors.
      • Relationships like “king – man + woman ≈ queen” can emerge.

  • Context sensitivity:

    • Early embeddings (like Word2Vec) give one vector per word, regardless of context.
    • Newer models (like BERT) give different vectors for the same word in different sentences, capturing nuanced meaning.

  • Examples:

    • Word2Vec:
      • Learns embeddings by predicting a word from its context (CBOW) or context from a word (Skip-gram).
    • BERT (by Google):
      • A transformer-based model.
      • Reads entire sentences bidirectionally.
      • Produces context-aware embeddings for each token.

So you can think of it like this: - Bag of words: counts, no order, no context. - Embeddings: numbers that encode meaning and context, learned from data.

Language specifics and limitations

The text then zooms out to say: “We’re focusing on English, but the world is bigger.”

Why English first?

  • Reason:
    • Many tools, examples, and packages are built around English.
    • English has relatively simple morphology (compared to some languages) and uses spaces between words, which makes tokenization easier.
  • Proto-Indo-European languages:
    • Many European languages share structural similarities:
      • Word order, alphabet, word boundaries, etc.
    • Techniques used for English often transfer reasonably well to languages like Spanish, French, German, etc.—with adjustments.

Why other languages can be harder

  • Challenges:
    • No spaces between words (e.g., Chinese, Japanese, Thai):
      • Tokenization itself becomes a non-trivial task.
    • Rich morphology (e.g., many forms of a word in Finnish, Turkish, some Indic languages):
      • Stemming/lemmatization is more complex.
    • Different scripts (e.g., Devanagari, Hangul, Arabic):
      • Requires Unicode-aware tools and language-specific rules.
  • Examples of language-specific tools:
    • Chinese:
      • Specialized toolkits for Chinese NLP and speech processing in R.
    • Indic languages (Hindi, Bengali, etc.):
      • Indic NLP libraries (often in Python).
    • Korean:
      • {KoNLP} package in R.
    • Multilingual NLP in R:
      • {udpipe} supports many languages with pre-trained models.
  • Multilingual BERT:
    • A version of BERT trained on many languages.
    • Helps bring powerful contextual embeddings to non-English text.
    • Still, performance varies by language and domain.

How this all fits together in your workflow

If we connect the dots:

  1. Start with raw text
    • Stored as strings in a data frame or as a corpus.
  2. Choose a representation:
    • For tidyverse-style analysis: convert to Tidy Text Format (one token per row).
    • For machine learning models: maybe convert to a DTM or use embeddings.
  3. Decide your token:
    • Words, n-grams, sentences, etc., depending on your question.
  4. Pick your level of sophistication:
    • Bag of words for simple, interpretable analysis (counts, tf–idf, basic sentiment).
    • Embeddings / BERT for deeper, context-aware modeling.
  5. Remember language matters:
    • English is the starting point here.
    • Other languages may need specialized tokenizers, models, and packages.

General Concepts and Language Specifics in Text Analysis

Why the focus is on English

English is the starting point for most introductory text‑analysis work because:

  • It uses spaces between words, making tokenization straightforward.
  • It has relatively simple morphology (few inflections compared to languages like Turkish or Finnish).
  • Most NLP tools, datasets, and tutorials were historically developed for English first.

This makes English the easiest language for demonstrating foundational concepts such as tokenization, stemming, lemmatization, sentiment analysis, and bag‑of‑words modeling.

How well do these techniques transfer to other languages?

Many European languages—especially those descended from Proto‑Indo‑European (e.g., Spanish, French, German, Italian)—share structural similarities with English:

  • Alphabetic writing systems
  • Word boundaries marked by spaces
  • Similar grammatical categories

Because of this, the same text‑analysis techniques often work with only minor adjustments.

However, once you move beyond these languages, things get more complex.

Why other languages can be more challenging

Languages differ dramatically in:

1. Tokenization

Some languages do not use spaces between words:

  • Chinese
  • Japanese
  • Thai

Tokenization becomes a non‑trivial problem requiring specialized algorithms.

2. Morphology

Some languages have rich inflectional systems:

  • Turkish
  • Finnish
  • Arabic
  • Many Indic languages

A single root word may appear in dozens or hundreds of forms, making simple bag‑of‑words approaches less effective.

3. Script and encoding

Languages may use:

  • Logographic scripts (Chinese)
  • Abugidas (Hindi, Bengali)
  • Syllabaries (Japanese kana)
  • Alphabets with complex rules (Korean Hangul)

This requires Unicode‑aware processing and language‑specific tokenizers.

4. Data availability

Many languages are low‑resource, meaning:

  • Few labeled datasets
  • Limited pretrained models
  • Sparse linguistic tools

This makes NLP tasks harder and less accurate.
Springer

Tools for non‑English NLP

The text mentions several language‑specific toolkits:

Chinese NLP

  • Specialized tokenizers and speech‑processing tools are required because Chinese lacks whitespace boundaries.

Indic NLP (Python)

  • Handles languages like Hindi, Bengali, Tamil, etc.
  • Deals with complex morphology and scripts.

KoNLP (R)

  • Designed for Korean, which uses Hangul and has unique morphological rules.

udpipe (R)

  • Supports many languages using pretrained models.
  • Useful for tokenization, POS tagging, lemmatization, and dependency parsing.

These tools exist because English‑centric NLP methods do not generalize cleanly to all languages.

Multilingual NLP and modern solutions

Modern NLP research is rapidly improving multilingual capabilities.

Multilingual BERT (mBERT)

  • Trained on over 100 languages.
  • Learns shared representations across languages.
  • Helps with cross‑lingual tasks like translation, classification, and semantic similarity.
    shoutdigital.com

Cross‑lingual embeddings

  • Map words from different languages into a shared vector space.
  • Allow models trained in English to transfer knowledge to other languages.
    deep-learning-mit.github.io

Challenges remain

Even with multilingual models, issues persist:

  • Low‑resource languages still lag behind.
  • Morphological complexity reduces accuracy.
  • Cultural and contextual differences affect sentiment and meaning.
    fastdatascience.com

A Text Cleaning Workflow

1. Preserve the raw text unchanged

Idea: Treat raw text like raw data in any analysis—you never touch it.

  • Why keep an untouched copy?
    • Cleaning is destructive: once you remove accents, punctuation, or words, you can’t always reconstruct the original.
    • If you discover a mistake or want to try a different cleaning strategy, you need the original to start over.
  • Practical habit:
    • Store raw text in one object/column (e.g., text_raw).
    • Do all cleaning into a new object/column (e.g., text_clean).

This is about reversibility and trust—you always know you can go back.

2. Create a cleaned copy for analysis

Idea: All transformations happen on a copy, not the original.

  • You might:
    • Lowercase text.
    • Standardize Unicode.
    • Normalize punctuation.
    • Remove or transform characters.
  • Because it’s a copy, you’re free to experiment:
    • Try different tokenization strategies.
    • Compare results with and without transliteration.
    • Adjust stop-word lists.

This separation keeps your workflow safe and flexible.

3. Standardize Unicode representations

This is where things get subtle and “invisible.”

What is Unicode?

  • Unicode is a global standard that assigns a unique code point to each character across languages and symbol systems.
  • Example: the letter “é” can be represented in two ways:
    • Precomposed (NFC):
      • Single code point: U+00E9 → “LATIN SMALL LETTER E WITH ACUTE”
    • Decomposed (NFD):
      • Two code points:
        • U+0065 → “LATIN SMALL LETTER E”
        • U+0301 → “COMBINING ACUTE ACCENT”
  • Visually:
    • é (NFC)
    • é (NFD: e + combining accent)
      They look identical, but they are different sequences of characters.

Why this is a problem

These invisible differences can break your analysis:

  • String matching fails:
    • "é" != "é" if one is NFC and the other is NFD.
  • Tokenization issues:
    • The same apparent word might be split or treated as different tokens.
  • Joins and comparisons break silently:
    • Joining on text columns may miss matches that look identical.

What standardization does

  • Standardization converts all text to a single, consistent Unicode normalization form.
  • Common choice: NFC (Canonical Composition).
    • Accented characters are stored as single units.
    • Works well with word-level tokenization and most tools. RDocumentation stringi

In R with {stringi}

You might do:

text_nfc <- stringi::stri_trans_nfc(text_raw)

This:

  • Converts all strings to NFC.
  • Removes hidden encoding differences.
  • Makes later steps (matching, tokenizing, joining) more reliable.

Key distinction:
- Standardization (Step 3): fixes hidden encoding differences without changing what the text looks like.

4. Normalize and simplify the text (as appropriate)

Once Unicode is standardized, the text is internally consistent—but still stylistically varied.

What normalization is

Normalization is a deliberate simplification of text to reduce variation that can interfere with analysis.

Examples:

  • Punctuation and symbols:
    • Em dash — → hyphen -
    • Curly quotes “ ” → straight quotes "
    • Curly apostrophe ’ → straight apostrophe '
  • Accents (via transliteration):
    • “café” → “cafe”
    • “naïve” → “naive”

This helps:

  • Improve tokenization.
  • Reduce duplicate tokens that differ only by style.
  • Make matching and aggregation more robust.

But normalization can change meaning

Unlike Unicode standardization, normalization can alter the text:

  • Removing punctuation may remove clues about structure or emphasis.
  • Transliteration may collapse distinct words into the same form.

So normalization is context-dependent: - Great for frequency counts, rough clustering, or search. - Risky when exact spelling or nuance matters (e.g., legal text, names, linguistic research).

5. Transliteration and meaning

Transliteration is a specific kind of normalization: mapping characters to simpler equivalents, often ASCII.

Examples

  • “café” → “cafe”
  • “naïve” → “naive”

Helpful for:

  • Simplifying matching and counting.
  • Handling systems that only support ASCII.
  • Reducing noise from minor spelling variations.

But it can change semantics:

  • “résumé” → “resume”
    • résumé: document summarizing experience.
    • resume: to continue after a pause.
  • “exposé” → “expose”
    • exposé: a revealing report.
    • expose: the verb “to reveal”.

So:

  • Good for: exploratory analysis, word frequency, rough clustering.
  • Bad for: tasks where spelling distinctions matter (e.g., named entities, legal terms, linguistic nuance).

6. Example: Standardization and normalization with {stringi}

You’re given this example:

library(stringi)

text_raw <- c("café — “quoted text”", "caché isn’t the same as cache")

# Step 3: Standardize Unicode (NFC)
text_nfc <- stringi::stri_trans_nfc(text_raw)

# Step 4: Normalize punctuation and optionally transliterate
text_clean <- text_nfc |>
  stringi::stri_replace_all_fixed("—", "-") |>     # em dash → hyphen
  stringi::stri_replace_all_fixed("“", "\"") |>    # left quote → "
  stringi::stri_replace_all_fixed("”", "\"") |>    # right quote → "
  stringi::stri_replace_all_fixed("’", "'") |>     # curly apostrophe → '
  stringi::stri_trans_general("Latin-ASCII")       # transliteration

text_raw
[1] "café — “quoted text”"          "caché isn’t the same as cache"
# [1] "café — “quoted text”"          "caché isn’t the same as cache"

text_clean
[1] "cafe - \"quoted text\""        "cache isn't the same as cache"
# [1] "cafe - \"quoted text\""        "cache isn't the same as cache"

What happened?

  • Standardization (NFC):
    • Ensures all characters like “é” are in a consistent internal form.
  • Normalization:
    • — → -
    • “ and ” → "
    • ’ → '
    • Accented characters → ASCII equivalents ("café" → "cafe", "caché" → "cache")

Notice the semantic issue:

  • "caché isn’t the same as cache"
    becomes
  • "cache isn't the same as cache"

Now the sentence is logically false and loses its original point. That’s the cost of transliteration.

This is a perfect illustration of why you must choose normalization steps intentionally.

7. Tokenize the text

Once text is standardized and normalized (as needed), you tokenize it.

  • Tokenization: splitting text into meaningful units:
    • Words
    • N-grams (e.g., bigrams, trigrams)
    • Sentences
    • Paragraphs
  • Choice depends on your goal:
    • Word frequency → word tokens.
    • Phrase analysis → n-grams.
    • Sentiment over narrative flow → sentences or paragraphs.

In {tidytext}, this is often done with unnest_tokens().

8. Remove or customize stop words

Stop words: very common words like “the”, “and”, “is”.

  • Often removed to:
    • Focus on content words.
    • Reduce noise in frequency analysis or topic modeling.
  • But they’re not always “junk”:
    • In sentiment analysis, words like “not” are crucial.
    • In phrase detection, function words can matter.

So you might:

  • Start with a standard stop-word list.
  • Customize it:
    • Remove “not” from the stop list.
    • Add domain-specific words (e.g., “http”, “www”, “rt” in tweets).

Stop-word handling is not one-size-fits-all—it’s part of your modeling choices.

9. Document every cleaning choice

This is the part most people skip—and regret later.

  • Why document?
    • Every cleaning step changes the data.
    • Different choices (e.g., transliteration vs. not, stop words vs. none) can lead to different conclusions.
    • Reproducibility: you (or someone else) should be able to rerun the analysis and get the same results.

Good practice:

  • Keep your cleaning steps in a script, in order.
  • Comment why you made each decision.
  • Note especially:
    • Whether you transliterated.
    • Which stop-word list you used.
    • How you tokenized.

10. How this all fits into the bigger workflow

Putting it together, your text cleaning pipeline looks like:

  1. Keep raw text unchanged.
  2. Create a working copy for cleaning.
  3. Standardize Unicode (e.g., NFC) to remove hidden encoding differences.
  4. Normalize text (punctuation, quotes, dashes, optional transliteration) based on your goals.
  5. Tokenize into words, n-grams, sentences, etc.
  6. Handle stop words (remove, customize, or keep).
  7. Document every step for transparency and reproducibility.

Then, with {tidytext}, {stringr}, and {stringi}, you move from messy raw text to structured, analyzable data—ready for frequency analysis, sentiment, modeling, and visualization.

The {tidytext} package

1. What {tidytext} is for

{tidytext} is an R package that:

  • Turns text into tidy data (one token per row). juliasilge.github.io Text Mining with R
  • Plays nicely with {dplyr}, {ggplot2}, and the rest of the tidyverse.
  • Gives you tools for:
    • Tokenization (unnest_tokens()).
    • Sentiment analysis.
    • Working with stop words and lexicons.

You load it like this:

library(tidyverse)
── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
✔ ggplot2   3.5.2     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
✔ purrr     1.0.4     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(tidytext)

2. Starting point: a character vector of lines

You’re given the first stanza of Pablo Neruda’s If You Forget Me as a character vector:

text <- c(
  "If You Forget Me",
  "by Pablo Neruda",
  "I want you to know",
  "one thing.",
  "You know how this is:",
  "if I look",
  "at the crystal moon, at the red branch",
  "of the slow autumn at my window,",
  "if I touch",
  "near the fire",
  "the impalpable ash",
  "or the wrinkled body of the log,",
  "everything carries me to you,",
  "as if everything that exists,",
  "aromas, light, metals,",
  "were little boats",
  "that sail",
  "toward those isles of yours that wait for me."
)

This is:

  • A vector of length 18 (18 lines).
  • Each element is a line of text, not yet in tidy format.

3. Quick exploration with length(), map_dbl(), str_length(), str_count()

You’re asked to get some basic info:

3.1 Number of lines

length(text)
[1] 18
  • Returns 18 → 18 lines.

3.2 Number of characters per line

map_dbl(text, str_length)
 [1] 16 15 18 10 21  9 38 32 10 13 18 32 29 29 22 17  9 45
  • str_length() (from {stringr}) counts characters in each string.
  • map_dbl() (from {purrr}) applies str_length() to each element and returns a numeric vector.
  • You get a sense of line length variation.

3.3 Number of words per line

map_dbl(text, ~ str_count(., "\\w+"))
 [1] 4 3 5 2 5 3 8 7 3 3 3 7 5 5 3 3 2 9
  • str_count(., "\\w+") counts word-like sequences (letters/digits/underscore).
  • ~ defines an anonymous function; . is the current element.
  • This gives you words per line.

3.4 Total number of words

sum(map_dbl(text, ~ str_count(., "\\w+")))
[1] 80
  • Sums all word counts across lines.
  • You’re told the result is 80 words.

So at this point you know:

  • 18 lines.
  • 80 words total.
  • But this is still just a character vector, not a tibble, and definitely not one-token-per-row.

4. Why this is “not tidy text format”

Tidy text format means:

  • One token per row.
  • Stored in a tibble/data frame.
  • With other columns for metadata (e.g., line number, document ID).

Right now:

  • You have a vector, not a tibble.
  • Each element is a whole line, not individual tokens.
  • So you can’t easily:
    • Join with sentiment lexicons.
    • Count words with count(word, ...).
    • Group by line or other metadata.

You need to wrap this vector in a tibble and then tokenize.

5. Step 1: Convert the vector to a tibble with an index

You create a tibble with:

  • A line number column.
  • A text column.
text_df <- tibble(
  line = seq_len(length(text)),
  text = text
)
head(text_df, 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
    line text                                  
   <int> <chr>                                 
 1     1 If You Forget Me                      
 2     2 by Pablo Neruda                       
 3     3 I want you to know                    
 4     4 one thing.                            
 5     5 You know how this is:                 
 6     6 if I look                             
 7     7 at the crystal moon, at the red branch
 8     8 of the slow autumn at my window,      
 9     9 if I touch                            
10    10 near the fire

This gives something like:

line text
1 “If You Forget Me”
2 “by Pablo Neruda”
3 “I want you to know”
… …

Why this matters:

  • line preserves the original structure (which line each token came from).
  • This is a common pattern: add an index (line, sentence, document ID) so you can group or reconstruct later.

6. Step 2: Convert to tidy text with unnest_tokens()

Now you use unnest_tokens() from {tidytext} to go from:

  • One row per line → one row per token.

6.1 Basic usage

text_df |>
  unnest_tokens(
    output = word,
    input  = text
  ) |>
  head(10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
    line word  
   <int> <chr> 
 1     1 if    
 2     1 you   
 3     1 forget
 4     1 me    
 5     2 by    
 6     2 pablo 
 7     2 neruda
 8     3 i     
 9     3 want  
10     3 you

Key arguments:

  • tbl: the input tibble (text_df), usually passed via the pipe.
  • output: name of the new column that will hold tokens (here, word).
  • input: the column containing the original text (here, text).
  • token (optional): what kind of token to create; default is "words".

Under the hood:

  • Each row’s text is split into words.
  • Each word becomes its own row.
  • The line column is repeated for each word from that line.

Result:

  • You now have 80 rows (one per word).
  • Columns: line, word.

6.2 Token types

unnest_tokens() supports many token types via token =:

  • "words" (default)
  • "characters"
  • "ngrams" (e.g., bigrams, trigrams)
  • "sentences"
  • "lines"
  • "paragraphs"
  • "regex" (custom splitting)
  • "tweets" (preserves usernames, hashtags, URLs)
  • "ptb" (Penn Treebank tokenization)

This is how you control the granularity of your analysis.

6.3 Defaults: lowercase and punctuation

By default, unnest_tokens():

  • Strips punctuation.
  • Converts to lowercase.

So:

  • "You know how this is:"
    becomes tokens like "you", "know", "how", "this", "is".

If you want to keep case, use:

text_df |>
  unnest_tokens(
    output = word,
    input  = text,
    to_lower = FALSE
  )
# A tibble: 80 × 2
    line word  
   <int> <chr> 
 1     1 If    
 2     1 You   
 3     1 Forget
 4     1 Me    
 5     2 by    
 6     2 Pablo 
 7     2 Neruda
 8     3 I     
 9     3 want  
10     3 you   
# ℹ 70 more rows

7. Step 3: Remove stop words with anti_join(stop_words)

Now you have 80 word tokens, but many are very common words:

  • “I”, “the”, “and”, “or”, “if”, “you”, etc.

These are stop words—often not useful for certain analyses (like topic modeling or simple frequency analysis).

7.1 Load built-in stop words

{tidytext} includes a data frame called stop_words:

data(stop_words)
  • Contains 1,149 stop words from three lexicons. juliasilge.github.io The Comprehensive R Archive Network
  • Column word holds the stop word itself.

7.2 Remove stop words with anti_join()

You:

text_df |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>  # remove rows whose 'word' is in stop_words
  count(word, sort = TRUE) ->            # count remaining words
  text_word_count

What happens:

  1. unnest_tokens(word, text)
    • One row per word, 80 rows total.
  2. anti_join(stop_words, by = "word")
    • Keeps only rows where word is not in stop_words$word.
    • This is a filtering join: it removes “uninteresting” common words.
  3. count(word, sort = TRUE)
    • Groups by word and counts occurrences.
    • Sorts by frequency (most common first).
    • Result is a tibble with columns: word, n.
  4. Assignment to text_word_count
    • Stores the result for later use.

7.3 How many words are left?

nrow(text_word_count)
[1] 26
  • You’re told: 26 rows instead of 80.
  • That means:
    • 80 total tokens.
    • After removing stop words and collapsing duplicates via count(), you have 26 unique, non-stop words.

These are the words that carry more semantic weight in this stanza.

8. The core workflow you just practiced

You’ve basically walked through the minimal tidy text pipeline:

  1. Convert raw text to a tibble
    • One row per unit (line, document, etc.).
    • Add an index column (line) to preserve structure.
  2. Convert to tidy text format with unnest_tokens()
    • One row per token (usually word).
    • Keep metadata columns (like line) alongside tokens.
  3. Remove stop words (if appropriate)
    • Use stop_words and anti_join() to filter out very common words.
    • This sharpens your focus on content-bearing terms.
  4. Summarize
    • Use count(word, sort = TRUE) to see which words dominate.
  5. Save to a new tibble
    • text_word_count is now your cleaned, token-level data ready for:
      • Frequency plots.
      • Word clouds.
      • Further analysis (e.g., sentiment, n-grams).

Tidytext Example 2: Jane Austen’s Novels with {janeaustenr}

This example scales up from a small poem to a full literary corpus—all six major Jane Austen novels. It shows how tidy text methods handle large, structured text.

The {janeaustenr} package provides:

  • Clean, pre‑processed text from Project Gutenberg
  • A consistent format across books
  • Metadata (book titles)

This makes it perfect for learning text mining without worrying about messy raw data.

Load the data

library(janeaustenr)

The function:

austen_books()
# A tibble: 73,422 × 2
   text                    book               
 * <chr>                   <fct>              
 1 "SENSE AND SENSIBILITY" Sense & Sensibility
 2 ""                      Sense & Sensibility
 3 "by Jane Austen"        Sense & Sensibility
 4 ""                      Sense & Sensibility
 5 "(1811)"                Sense & Sensibility
 6 ""                      Sense & Sensibility
 7 ""                      Sense & Sensibility
 8 ""                      Sense & Sensibility
 9 ""                      Sense & Sensibility
10 "CHAPTER 1"             Sense & Sensibility
# ℹ 73,412 more rows

returns a tibble with:

column meaning
text Lines of text (≈70 characters each)
book Factor indicating which novel the line belongs to

This is your corpus.

Inspect how chapters are marked

Before adding metadata, you need to understand how chapters appear in the text.

austen_books() |> head(20)
# A tibble: 20 × 2
   text                                                                    book 
   <chr>                                                                   <fct>
 1 "SENSE AND SENSIBILITY"                                                 Sens…
 2 ""                                                                      Sens…
 3 "by Jane Austen"                                                        Sens…
 4 ""                                                                      Sens…
 5 "(1811)"                                                                Sens…
 6 ""                                                                      Sens…
 7 ""                                                                      Sens…
 8 ""                                                                      Sens…
 9 ""                                                                      Sens…
10 "CHAPTER 1"                                                             Sens…
11 ""                                                                      Sens…
12 ""                                                                      Sens…
13 "The family of Dashwood had long been settled in Sussex.  Their estate" Sens…
14 "was large, and their residence was at Norland Park, in the centre of"  Sens…
15 "their property, where, for many generations, they had lived in so"     Sens…
16 "respectable a manner as to engage the general good opinion of their"   Sens…
17 "surrounding acquaintance.  The late owner of this estate was a single" Sens…
18 "man, who lived to a very advanced age, and who for many years of his"  Sens…
19 "life, had a constant companion and housekeeper in his sister.  But he… Sens…
20 "death, which happened ten years before his own, produced a great"      Sens…

Then search for lines that begin with “chapter”:

austen_books() |>
  filter(str_detect(text, "(?i)^chapter")) |> 
  slice_sample(n = 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   text          book               
   <chr>         <fct>              
 1 CHAPTER XXXIV Mansfield Park     
 2 CHAPTER 4     Northanger Abbey   
 3 CHAPTER VII   Mansfield Park     
 4 CHAPTER XXI   Mansfield Park     
 5 Chapter 14    Persuasion         
 6 CHAPTER IV    Emma               
 7 Chapter 8     Pride & Prejudice  
 8 CHAPTER 41    Sense & Sensibility
 9 CHAPTER III   Emma               
10 CHAPTER IX    Emma

What you learn:

  • Chapters always begin with the word chapter
  • Case may vary: CHAPTER, Chapter, chapter
  • Numbers may be Arabic (e.g., “1”) or Roman numerals (e.g., “XIV”)

This lets you build a chapter counter.

Add metadata: book, chapter, line number

This is a crucial step. You’re preparing the text so that after tokenization you can still:

  • Reconstruct where each word came from
  • Analyze by book
  • Analyze by chapter
  • Analyze by line

Code:

austen_books() |>
  group_by(book) |>
  mutate(
    linenumber = row_number(),
    chapter = cumsum(str_detect(
      text,
      regex("^chapter [\\divxlc]", ignore_case = TRUE)
    )),
    .before = text
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(book, chapter, linenumber, text) ->
orig_books

What each part does:

group_by(book)

Ensures line numbers and chapter counts restart for each novel.

linenumber = row_number()

Adds a sequential line index within each book.

chapter = cumsum(str_detect(...))
  • str_detect() returns TRUE when a line starts with “chapter …”
  • cumsum() turns TRUE/FALSE into a running chapter count
    (each TRUE increments the chapter number)
regex(..., ignore_case = TRUE)

Makes the pattern case‑insensitive.

.before = text

Places the new columns before the text column.

ungroup()

Removes grouping so later operations don’t accidentally group by book.

select(book, chapter, linenumber, text)

Keeps only the relevant columns.

Result:

  • 73,422 lines
  • ~730,000 total words (non‑unique)

This is your structured corpus.

Convert to tidy text format

Now you tokenize the text into one word per row.

orig_books |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>   # ~725,055 rows
  mutate(word = str_extract(word, "[a-z']+")) |> 
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") ->
tidy_books

Let’s break this down.

Step A: unnest_tokens(word, text)

This:

  • Splits each line into words
  • Removes punctuation
  • Converts to lowercase
  • Produces one row per word
  • Keeps metadata columns (book, chapter, linenumber)

You now have 725,055 rows—one per token.

Step B: Clean formatting characters

Project Gutenberg uses underscores to mark italics:

  • _happy_
  • _very_important_

str_extract(word, "[a-z']+") pulls out only:

  • lowercase letters
  • apostrophes

So _happy_ → happy

Step C: Remove stop words

anti_join(stop_words, by = "word")

This removes extremely common words like:

  • the
  • and
  • of
  • to
  • I

These words dominate frequency counts but rarely add meaning.

Inspect the cleaned tidy text

Number of rows:

nrow(tidy_books)
[1] 216385

Result:

  • 216,385 tokens
    (after removing stop words and formatting)

Number of unique words:

length(unique(tidy_books$word))
[1] 13464

Result:

  • 13,464 unique words

This is your vocabulary.

Count word frequencies

tidy_books |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE)
# A tibble: 13,464 × 2
   word       n
   <chr>  <int>
 1 miss    1860
 2 time    1339
 3 fanny    862
 4 dear     822
 5 lady     819
 6 sir      807
 7 day      797
 8 emma     787
 9 sister   727
10 house    699
# ℹ 13,454 more rows

This gives:

  • Each unique word
  • How many times it appears across all six novels
  • Sorted from most to least frequent

This is the foundation for:

  • Word clouds
  • Zipf’s law analysis
  • TF‑IDF
  • Topic modeling
  • Sentiment analysis

Plot the most common words

Basic plot (words with > 500 occurrences)

tidy_books |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  filter(n > 500) |>
  mutate(word = fct_reorder(word, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(word, n)) +
  geom_col() +
  xlab(NULL) +
  coord_flip()

Example

*Part I — Plot the Most Common Words**

Problem (Part I)

Using the tidy version of Jane Austen’s novels (tidy_books), plot the most common words (defined as words appearing more than 500 times) in descending order, using a simple bar chart.

*Solution (Part I)**

tidy_books |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  filter(n > 500) |>
  mutate(word = fct_reorder(word, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(word, n)) +
  geom_col() +
  xlab(NULL) +
  coord_flip()

Explanation (Part I)

1. Count words

count(word, sort = TRUE)

Creates a frequency table of all words across all six novels.

2. Filter to “most common”

filter(n > 500)

Keeps only words appearing more than 500 times.

3. Reorder factor levels

mutate(word = fct_reorder(word, n))

Ensures the bars appear in descending order.

4. Plot

  • geom_col() makes a bar chart
  • coord_flip() rotates it horizontally for readability

This gives a clean visualization of Austen’s most frequent words.

What this reveals:

  • Which words are common across all novels
  • Which words are more characteristic of specific books
  • Stylistic patterns in Austen’s writing

What you’ve accomplished

You’ve now seen the full tidy‑text workflow applied to a large literary corpus:

Load text

Add metadata (book, chapter, line)

Tokenize into words

####Clean formatting
#### Remove stop words
#### Count frequencies
#### Visualize results

This is the exact same workflow used for:

  • Social media analysis
  • Customer reviews
  • News articles
  • Scientific papers
  • Chat logs
  • Historical documents

Once you understand this pattern, you can analyze any text dataset.

Part II — Words Unique to Each Book**

Problem (Part II)

Find the words that:

  1. Occur in only one of Jane Austen’s books
  2. And within each book, find the most frequent such word

You are encouraged to use:

  • pivot_wider()
  • pivot_longer()
  • across()
  • c_across()
  • The magrittr pipe (%>% or |>)

Solution (Part II)

tidy_books |>
  group_by(book) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = book, values_from = n) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), is.na, .names = "na_{ .col}")) |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(tot_books = sum(c_across(starts_with("na")))) |>
  ungroup() |> 
  filter(tot_books == max(tot_books)) |>
  select(!(starts_with("na_") | starts_with("tot"))) |>
  pivot_longer(-word,
    names_to = "book", values_to = "count",
    values_drop_na = TRUE
  ) |>
  group_by(book) |>
  filter(count == max(count)) |>
  arrange(desc(count))
# A tibble: 6 × 3
# Groups:   book [6]
  word     book                count
  <chr>    <chr>               <int>
1 elinor   Sense & Sensibility   623
2 crawford Mansfield Park        493
3 weston   Emma                  389
4 darcy    Pride & Prejudice     374
5 elliot   Persuasion            254
6 tilney   Northanger Abbey      196

Explanation (Part II)

1. Count words within each book

group_by(book) |> count(word)

Creates a table of word frequencies per book.

2. Pivot wider

pivot_wider(names_from = book, values_from = n)

Creates a structure like:

word Emma Persuasion MansfieldPark …

Words missing from a book become NA.

3. Detect which books a word does not appear in

mutate(across(where(is.numeric), is.na, .names = "na_{.col}"))

Creates TRUE/FALSE columns:

  • na_Emma
  • na_Persuasion
  • …

TRUE = word does not appear in that book.

4. Count how many books the word is missing from

rowwise() |> mutate(tot_books = sum(c_across(starts_with("na"))))

If there are 6 books:

  • A word appearing in only one book → tot_books = 5
  • A word appearing in all books → tot_books = 0

5. Filter to words appearing in only one book

filter(tot_books == max(tot_books))

6. Remove helper columns

select(!(starts_with("na_") | starts_with("tot")))

7. Pivot back to long format

pivot_longer(-word, names_to = "book", values_to = "count", values_drop_na = TRUE)

Now you have:

word | book | count |

Only for the book where the word appears.

8. For each book, keep the most frequent unique word

group_by(book) |> filter(count == max(count))

This gives the top unique word for each novel.

Part III — What if You Don’t Know How Many Books There Are?

Problem (Part III)

Modify your approach so it works even if:

  • You don’t know how many books there are
  • Or there are many books
  • Or the book names are unknown

Good news:

The solution from Part II already works for any number of books.

Why?

  • pivot_wider() automatically creates one column per book
  • across(where(is.numeric)) automatically selects all book columns
  • starts_with("na_") automatically finds all NA‑indicator columns
  • pivot_longer() automatically returns to tidy format

So the solution for Part III is identical:

Solution (Part III)

(same as Part II)

tidy_books |>
  group_by(book) |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = book, values_from = n) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), is.na, .names = "na_{ .col}")) |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(tot_books = sum(c_across(starts_with("na")))) |>
  ungroup() |> 
  filter(tot_books == max(tot_books)) |>
  select(!(starts_with("na_") | starts_with("tot"))) |>
  pivot_longer(-word,
    names_to = "book", values_to = "count",
    values_drop_na = TRUE
  ) |>
  group_by(book) |>
  filter(count == max(count)) |>
  arrange(desc(count))
# A tibble: 6 × 3
# Groups:   book [6]
  word     book                count
  <chr>    <chr>               <int>
1 elinor   Sense & Sensibility   623
2 crawford Mansfield Park        493
3 weston   Emma                  389
4 darcy    Pride & Prejudice     374
5 elliot   Persuasion            254
6 tilney   Northanger Abbey      196

Summary of All Parts

Part Task Key Skills
I Plot most common words count(), filter(), ggplot(), fct_reorder()
II Find words unique to each book pivot_wider(), across(), c_across(), pivot_longer()
III Make solution scalable to unknown number of books Dynamic column selection, no hard‑coding

Below is a structured Compare Frequencies Across Authors* task.

  1. The problem
  2. A clean, reproducible workflow
  3. Full code for each step
  4. Why each step matters
  5. How to interpret the final plots and correlations

This is designed to match the style of the earlier Parts I–III solutions.

Compare Word Frequencies Across Authors

We want to compare Jane Austen to:

  • The Brontë Sisters (Charlotte, Emily, Anne)
  • H. G. Wells

Specifically, we want to compare relative word frequencies (non‑stop words) across authors.

This is a classic cross‑corpus comparison problem.

Overall Strategy

  1. Download books from Project Gutenberg using {gutenbergr}
  2. Standardize and normalize text
  3. Tokenize into tidy text format
  4. Remove stop words and formatting artifacts
  5. Add an author column
  6. Combine all authors into one tibble
  7. Compute relative frequencies
  8. Reshape for comparison
  9. Plot Austen vs Brontë and Austen vs Wells
  10. Compute correlations

Step 1 — Identify and Download Books

We’ll use {gutenbergr} to download:

Jane Austen

(Already provided by {janeaustenr}, but we can keep her as-is.)

H. G. Wells

  • The Time Machine
  • The War of the Worlds
  • The Invisible Man
  • The Island of Doctor Moreau

The Brontë Sisters

  • Jane Eyre
  • Wuthering Heights
  • Agnes Grey
  • The Tenant of Wildfell Hall
  • Villette

We’ll use Gutenberg IDs.

Step 2 — Standardize and Normalize Text

Because Gutenberg text is messy, we apply:

  • Unicode standardization (NFC)
  • Normalization of punctuation
  • Optional transliteration
  • Removal of formatting characters (underscores, asterisks, etc.)

This ensures tokenization and matching work correctly.

Step 3 — Tokenize and Clean

We:

  • Use unnest_tokens()
  • Extract only alphabetic words
  • Remove stop words
  • Add an author column

Step 4 — Combine All Authors

We bind the three author tibbles into one.

Step 5 — Compute Relative Frequencies

For each author:

[ = ]

This allows fair comparison across authors with different corpus sizes.

Step 6 — Reshape for Comparison

We:

  • pivot_wider() to create columns:
    • austen
    • bronte
    • wells
  • pivot_longer() to combine Brontë and Wells into a single author column
  • This allows faceting in ggplot

Step 7 — Plot Austen vs Others

Scatter plots:

  • x‑axis: Austen relative frequency
  • y‑axis: Other author’s relative frequency
  • geom_abline() shows the line of equality
  • facet_wrap(~author) gives two panels:
    • Austen vs Brontë
    • Austen vs Wells

Step 8 — Correlation Tests

Use:

cor.test(austen, other_author)

This quantifies similarity in vocabulary usage.

FULL WORKED SOLUTION

Below is the complete code, fully annotated.

Load Packages

library(tidyverse)
library(tidytext)
library(janeaustenr)
library(gutenbergr)
Warning: package 'gutenbergr' was built under R version 4.5.3
library(stringi)
library(stringr)

Download and Prepare Text

Helper function to clean Gutenberg text

clean_gutenberg <- function(gid, author_name) {
  gutenberg_download(gid) |>
    mutate(
      text = stri_trans_nfc(text),              # Unicode standardization
      text = str_replace_all(text, "—", "-"),   # normalize punctuation
      text = str_replace_all(text, "[“”]", "\""),
      text = str_replace_all(text, "’", "'"),
      text = stri_trans_general(text, "Latin-ASCII")
    ) |>
    unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
    mutate(word = str_extract(word, "[a-z']+")) |>
    filter(!is.na(word)) |>
    anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
    mutate(author = author_name)
}

Download Books

H. G. Wells

wells_ids <- c(35, 36, 5230, 159)  # example Gutenberg IDs
wells <- map_dfr(wells_ids, clean_gutenberg, author_name = "Wells")
Using mirror https://aleph.pglaf.org.
wells %>% 
  head
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  gutenberg_id word         author
         <int> <chr>        <chr> 
1           35 time         Wells 
2           35 machine      Wells 
3           35 invention    Wells 
4           35 contents     Wells 
5           35 introduction Wells 
6           35 ii           Wells

Brontë Sisters

bronte_ids <- c(1260, 768, 767, 9182, 918) 
bronte <- map_dfr(bronte_ids, clean_gutenberg, author_name = "Bronte")
bronte %>% 
head()
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  gutenberg_id word          author
         <int> <chr>         <chr> 
1         1260 jane          Bronte
2         1260 eyre          Bronte
3         1260 autobiography Bronte
4         1260 charlotte     Bronte
5         1260 bronte        Bronte
6         1260 illustrated   Bronte

Jane Austen (already clean)

austen <- austen_books() |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  mutate(word = str_extract(word, "[a-z']+")) |>
  filter(!is.na(word)) |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  mutate(author = "Austen")
austen %>% 
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 3
  book                word        author
  <fct>               <chr>       <chr> 
1 Sense & Sensibility sense       Austen
2 Sense & Sensibility sensibility Austen
3 Sense & Sensibility jane        Austen
4 Sense & Sensibility austen      Austen
5 Sense & Sensibility chapter     Austen
6 Sense & Sensibility family      Austen

Combine All Authors

all_authors <- bind_rows(austen, bronte, wells)
all_authors %>% 
  head()
# A tibble: 6 × 4
  book                word        author gutenberg_id
  <fct>               <chr>       <chr>         <int>
1 Sense & Sensibility sense       Austen           NA
2 Sense & Sensibility sensibility Austen           NA
3 Sense & Sensibility jane        Austen           NA
4 Sense & Sensibility austen      Austen           NA
5 Sense & Sensibility chapter     Austen           NA
6 Sense & Sensibility family      Austen           NA

Compute Relative Frequencies

freqs <- all_authors |>
  count(author, word) |>
  group_by(author) |>
  mutate(rel_freq = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup()

Reshape for Comparison

freq_wide <- freqs |>
  select(author, word, rel_freq) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = author, values_from = rel_freq)

Now we have:

word | Austen | Bronte | Wells |

Pivot Longer for Faceting

freq_long <- freq_wide |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Bronte, Wells),
    names_to = "other_author",
    values_to = "other_freq"
  )

Now we have:

word | Austen | other_author | other_freq |

Plot Austen vs Brontë and Austen vs Wells

freq_long |>
  ggplot(aes(Austen, other_freq)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~other_author, scales = "free") +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(
    x = "Austen Relative Frequency",
    y = "Other Author Relative Frequency",
    title = "Comparison of Word Frequencies Across Authors"
  )
Warning: Removed 40077 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).

Why log scales?

  • Word frequencies follow Zipf’s law
  • Log‑log plots reveal structure more clearly

Correlation Tests

freq_long |>
  group_by(other_author) |>
  summarise(
    correlation = cor(Austen, other_freq, use = "complete.obs"),
    p_value = cor.test(Austen, other_freq)$p.value
  )
# A tibble: 2 × 3
  other_author correlation   p_value
  <chr>              <dbl>     <dbl>
1 Bronte             0.748 0        
2 Wells              0.423 6.07e-259

This gives:

  • Correlation between Austen and Brontë
  • Correlation between Austen and Wells

How to Interpret the Plots

1. Points near the red line

→ Words used at similar rates by both authors.

2. Points above the line

→ Words used more often by Brontë/Wells than Austen.

3. Points below the line

→ Words Austen uses more frequently.

4. Tight clustering

→ Authors have similar vocabularies.

5. Wide scatter

→ Authors differ stylistically.

Typically:

  • Austen vs Brontë → higher correlation (similar genre, era)
  • Austen vs Wells → lower correlation (sci‑fi vs Regency romance)

You now have a complete, end‑to‑end workflow

This is a full professional‑grade text‑analysis pipeline:

  • Data acquisition
  • Cleaning
  • Unicode standardization
  • Normalization
  • Tokenization
  • Stop‑word removal
  • Frequency analysis
  • Reshaping
  • Visualization
  • Statistical comparison

Sentiment Analysis — Complete Explanation + All R Code

Overview — What Sentiment Analysis Is

When humans read text, we naturally infer emotional tone:

  • Is the passage positive or negative?
  • Does it express fear, joy, anger, trust, sadness, etc.?
  • Does the author imply emotion indirectly (“showing not saying”)?

Sentiment Analysis (aka opinion mining) tries to do this computationally.

Common uses:

  • Customer reviews
  • Social media monitoring
  • Product feedback
  • Market intelligence
  • Political messaging analysis

Simplest method: Bag‑of‑Words Sentiment

  1. Tokenize text into words
  2. Look up each word in a sentiment lexicon
  3. Add up positive and negative words

Limitations of Bag‑of‑Words

  • Ignores negation:
    • “not good” → counts as “good” (positive)
  • Ignores context
  • Positive and negative words cancel out over long text
  • Works better on sentences or paragraphs than entire books

Sentiment Lexicons

Sentiment lexicons assign emotional labels or scores to words.

Why multiple lexicons?

Because:

  • Words change meaning over time (semantic drift)
  • Different domains use words differently
  • Some lexicons focus on emotions, others on polarity
  • Some are numeric, others categorical

Lexicons in {tidytext}

{tidytext} provides access to three major lexicons (via {textdata}):

1. Bing Liu lexicon (“bing”)

  • Labels each word as positive or negative
  • Good for simple polarity analysis

2. AFINN lexicon

  • Assigns integer scores from –5 to +5
  • Captures intensity
    • “terrible” = –3
    • “excellent” = +5

3. NRC lexicon

  • Assigns 10 emotions:
    • anger
    • anticipation
    • disgust
    • fear
    • joy
    • negative
    • positive
    • sadness
    • surprise
    • trust
  • A word may have multiple sentiments

Accessing Sentiments in {tidytext}

Load {textdata}

library(textdata)

Explore the built‑in sentiments data frame

sentiments |>
  arrange(word) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   word        sentiment
   <chr>       <chr>    
 1 2-faces     negative 
 2 abnormal    negative 
 3 abolish     negative 
 4 abominable  negative 
 5 abominably  negative 
 6 abominate   negative 
 7 abomination negative 
 8 abort       negative 
 9 aborted     negative 
10 aborts      negative

Load each lexicon

get_sentiments("bing") |> slice_sample(n = 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   word          sentiment
   <chr>         <chr>    
 1 heedless      negative 
 2 displeased    negative 
 3 well-regarded positive 
 4 inadequate    negative 
 5 suck          negative 
 6 scummy        negative 
 7 terrorize     negative 
 8 hissing       negative 
 9 strongest     positive 
10 spookily      negative 
get_sentiments("afinn") |> slice_sample(n = 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   word       value
   <chr>      <dbl>
 1 enslaves      -2
 2 risks         -2
 3 luck           3
 4 mistakes      -2
 5 complained    -2
 6 reassures      1
 7 tumor         -2
 8 drained       -2
 9 stricken      -2
10 celebrated     3
get_sentiments("nrc") |> slice_sample(n = 10)
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   word           sentiment   
   <chr>          <chr>       
 1 withdraw       negative    
 2 deplore        negative    
 3 obstacle       negative    
 4 boomerang      anticipation
 5 gift           surprise    
 6 procession     sadness     
 7 aspiring       joy         
 8 cardiomyopathy sadness     
 9 unfairness     negative    
10 snob           negative

List NRC sentiment categories

unique(get_sentiments("nrc")$sentiment) |> sort()
 [1] "anger"        "anticipation" "disgust"      "fear"         "joy"         
 [6] "negative"     "positive"     "sadness"      "surprise"     "trust"

How many NRC words have multiple sentiments?

get_sentiments("nrc") |>
  group_by(word) |>
  summarize(nums = n()) |>
  filter(nums > 1) |>
  nrow() / nrow(get_sentiments("nrc"))
[1] 0.2654268

Example of a word with multiple sentiments

get_sentiments("nrc") |>
  filter(word == "feeling")
# A tibble: 10 × 2
   word    sentiment   
   <chr>   <chr>       
 1 feeling anger       
 2 feeling anticipation
 3 feeling disgust     
 4 feeling fear        
 5 feeling joy         
 6 feeling negative    
 7 feeling positive    
 8 feeling sadness     
 9 feeling surprise    
10 feeling trust

Count total NRC entries vs unique words

nrow(get_sentiments("nrc"))
[1] 13872
get_sentiments("nrc") |> select(word) |> unique() |> nrow()
[1] 6453

Example: Using NRC “Fear” Words

We will analyze fear‑related words in Jane Austen’s novels.

Step 1 — Convert Austen books to tidy text

austen_books() |>
  group_by(book) |>
  mutate(
    linenumber = row_number(),
    chapter = cumsum(str_detect(
      text,
      regex("^chapter [\\divxlc]", ignore_case = TRUE)
    ))
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  unnest_tokens(output = word, input = text) ->
tidy_books

Step 2 — Extract only NRC “fear” words

get_sentiments("nrc") |>
  filter(sentiment == "fear") ->
nrcfear

Step 3 — Count fear words in Emma

tidy_books |>
  filter(book == "Emma") |>
  inner_join(nrcfear, by = "word", relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  count(word, sort = TRUE)
# A tibble: 364 × 2
   word         n
   <chr>    <int>
 1 doubt       98
 2 ill         72
 3 afraid      65
 4 marry       63
 5 change      61
 6 bad         60
 7 feeling     56
 8 bear        52
 9 creature    39
10 obliging    34
# ℹ 354 more rows

Step 4 — Count how many words belong to each NRC sentiment

get_sentiments("nrc") |>
  group_by(sentiment) |>
  count()
# A tibble: 10 × 2
# Groups:   sentiment [10]
   sentiment        n
   <chr>        <int>
 1 anger         1245
 2 anticipation   837
 3 disgust       1056
 4 fear          1474
 5 joy            687
 6 negative      3316
 7 positive      2308
 8 sadness       1187
 9 surprise       532
10 trust         1230

Exercise: Plot Fear Words per Chapter

Plot the number of fear words in each chapter for each Jane Austen book.

  • Consider using scales = "free_x" in facet_wrap(). #### Solution
tidy_books |>
  inner_join(nrcfear, by = "word", relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  group_by(book, chapter) |>
  count() ->
fear_chapter

head(fear_chapter)
# A tibble: 6 × 3
# Groups:   book, chapter [6]
  book                chapter     n
  <fct>                 <int> <int>
1 Sense & Sensibility       1    21
2 Sense & Sensibility       2    12
3 Sense & Sensibility       3    21
4 Sense & Sensibility       4    27
5 Sense & Sensibility       5    14
6 Sense & Sensibility       6     8

You would then plot:

fear_chapter |>
  ggplot(aes(chapter, n)) +
  geom_col() +
  facet_wrap(~book, scales = "free_x")

Positive vs Negative Sentiment in Larger Blocks (An Explanation of the code)

We break text into blocks of 80 lines and compute sentiment using bing.

Step 1 — Join tidy text with bing lexicon

tidy_books |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word",
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>

Step 2 — Count sentiment words per block

  count(book, index = linenumber %/% 80, sentiment) |>

Step 3 — Spread positive/negative into columns

  pivot_wider(
    names_from = sentiment, values_from = n,
    values_fill = list(n = 0)
  ) |>

Step 4 — Compute net sentiment

  mutate(net = positive - negative) ->
janeaustensentiment

Step 5 — Plot

janeaustensentiment |>
  ggplot(aes(index, net, fill = book)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~book, ncol = 2, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(end = .9)
### ---------------------------------------------------------
### PART 1 — FEAR WORDS PER CHAPTER (NRC "fear")
### ---------------------------------------------------------

# Step 1: Extract NRC fear words
nrcfear <- get_sentiments("nrc") |>
  filter(sentiment == "fear")

# Step 2: Join tidy_books with fear lexicon and count per chapter
fear_chapter <- tidy_books |>
  inner_join(nrcfear, by = "word", relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  group_by(book, chapter) |>
  count(name = "fear_count") |>
  ungroup()

# Step 3: Plot fear words per chapter
fear_chapter |>
  ggplot(aes(chapter, fear_count)) +
  geom_col() +
  facet_wrap(~book, scales = "free_x") +
  labs(
    title = "Fear Words per Chapter (NRC Lexicon)",
    x = "Chapter",
    y = "Count of Fear Words"
  )
### ---------------------------------------------------------
### PART 2 — POSITIVE vs NEGATIVE SENTIMENT IN 80-LINE BLOCKS
### ---------------------------------------------------------

# Step 1: Join tidy text with bing lexicon
# Step 2: Count sentiment words per 80-line block
# Step 3: Spread positive/negative into columns
# Step 4: Compute net sentiment
janeaustensentiment <- tidy_books |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word",
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  count(book, index = linenumber %/% 80, sentiment) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = sentiment,
    values_from = n,
    values_fill = list(n = 0)
  ) |>
  mutate(net = positive - negative)

# Step 5: Plot block-level sentiment
janeaustensentiment |>
  ggplot(aes(index, net, fill = book)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~book, ncol = 2, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(end = .9) +
  labs(
    title = "Net Sentiment in 80-Line Blocks (Bing Lexicon)",
    x = "Block Index (80 lines each)",
    y = "Net Sentiment (Positive - Negative)"
  )

Adjusting Sentiment Lexicons

Sometimes lexicons misclassify words.

Example:
In Jane Austen novels, “miss” is a title, not a negative verb.

Step 1 — Count most common sentiment words

tidy_books |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word",
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  count(word, sentiment, sort = TRUE) ->
bing_word_counts

bing_word_counts
# A tibble: 2,585 × 3
   word     sentiment     n
   <chr>    <chr>     <int>
 1 miss     negative   1855
 2 well     positive   1523
 3 good     positive   1380
 4 great    positive    981
 5 like     positive    725
 6 better   positive    639
 7 enough   positive    613
 8 happy    positive    534
 9 love     positive    495
10 pleasure positive    462
# ℹ 2,575 more rows

Step 2 — Plot top 10 positive/negative words

bing_word_counts |>
  group_by(sentiment) |>
  slice_max(order_by = n, n = 10) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(word = fct_reorder(word, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(word, n, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~sentiment, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(y = "Contribution to sentiment", x = NULL) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(end = .9)

This reveals the problem:
“miss” appears as a negative word, but in Austen it means “young woman.”

Fixing the Problem

Two approaches:

Approach 1 — Add “miss” to stop words

Step 1 — Create custom stop words

custom_stop_words <- bind_rows(
  tibble(word = c("miss"), lexicon = c("custom")),
  stop_words
)

Step 2 — Rebuild tidy_books without “miss”

austen_books() |>
  group_by(book) |>
  mutate(
    linenumber = row_number(),
    chapter = cumsum(str_detect(
      text,
      regex("^chapter [\\divxlc]", ignore_case = TRUE)
    ))
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  unnest_tokens(word, text) |>
  anti_join(custom_stop_words, by = "word") ->
tidy_books_no_miss

Step 3 — Recompute sentiment counts

tidy_books_no_miss |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word",
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  count(word, sentiment, sort = TRUE) ->
bing_word_counts

Step 4 — Plot again

bing_word_counts |>
  group_by(sentiment) |>
  slice_max(order_by = n, n = 10) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(word = fct_reorder(word, n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(word, n, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~sentiment, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(y = "Contribution to sentiment", x = NULL) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(end = .9)

Approach 2 — Remove “miss” from the lexicon

Step 1 — Modify bing lexicon

get_sentiments("bing") |>
  filter(word != "miss") ->
bing_no_miss

Step 2 — Recompute sentiment

tidy_books |>
  inner_join(bing_no_miss, by = "word",
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  count(word, sentiment, sort = TRUE) ->
bing_word_counts

Repeat the Plot by Chapter — Original vs “No Miss”

We now compare:

  • Original sentiment analysis using the bing lexicon
  • Adjusted sentiment analysis where the word “miss” has been removed from the lexicon

This lets us see how much a single misclassified word can distort sentiment patterns.

We will use {patchwork} to place the two plots side‑by‑side.

Load {patchwork}

library(patchwork)

Build the Original Sentiment Blocks (with “miss” counted as negative)

## Original
tidy_books |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word",
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  count(book, index = linenumber %/% 80, sentiment) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = sentiment, values_from = n,
    values_fill = list(n = 0)
  ) |>
  mutate(net = positive - negative) ->
janeaustensentiment

What this does:

  • Joins tidy text with bing lexicon
  • Counts positive/negative words in 80‑line blocks
  • Spreads into two columns: positive, negative
  • Computes net sentiment = positive − negative

Build the Adjusted Sentiment Blocks (with “miss” removed)

# No Miss
tidy_books |>
  inner_join(bing_no_miss, by = "word",
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  count(book, index = linenumber %/% 80, sentiment) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = sentiment, values_from = n,
    values_fill = list(n = 0)
  ) |>
  mutate(net = positive - negative) ->
janeaustensentiment2

What changed?

  • We use bing_no_miss instead of get_sentiments("bing")
  • Everything else is identical

Create the Two Plots

Plot 1 — Original

janeaustensentiment |>
  ggplot(aes(index, net, fill = book)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  ggtitle("With Miss as Negative") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(end = .9) +
  facet_wrap(~book, ncol = 2, scales = "free_x") -> p1

Plot 2 — Adjusted

janeaustensentiment2 |>
  ggplot(aes(index, net, fill = book)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  ggtitle("Without Miss as Negative") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(end = .9) +
  facet_wrap(~book, ncol = 2, scales = "free_x") -> p2

Combine the Plots with {patchwork}

p1 + p2

This displays the two sentiment trajectories side‑by‑side for direct comparison.

Compare the Average Net Sentiment

janeaustensentiment |>
  summarize(means = mean(net, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  bind_rows(
    (janeaustensentiment2 |>
      summarize(means = mean(net, na.rm = TRUE))
    )
  )
# A tibble: 2 × 1
  means
  <dbl>
1  9.71
2 11.7

Interpretation:

  • Removing “miss” makes the overall sentiment more positive
  • The difference is about 2 points on average
  • Some blocks (e.g., Emma block 110) change noticeably

This demonstrates how lexicon errors propagate into sentiment analysis.

Ordering Within Facets — {tidytext} Helpers

When faceting by both book and sentiment, simple fct_reorder() is not enough.
We need:

  • reorder_within()
  • scale_x_reordered()

These allow independent ordering within each facet.

Rebuild Word Counts Using the Adjusted Lexicon

tidy_books |>
  inner_join(bing_no_miss, by = "word",
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  count(word, sentiment, book, sort = TRUE) ->
bing_word_counts

Plot Top 5 Words per Book per Sentiment (Naive Version)

bing_word_counts |>
  group_by(book, sentiment) |>
  slice_max(order_by = n, n = 5) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(word = fct_reorder(parse_factor(word), n)) |>
  ggplot(aes(word, n, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(sentiment ~ book, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(
    y = "Contribution to sentiment",
    x = NULL
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(end = .9)

Problem:

  • Words are ordered globally, not within each facet
  • Negative words appear compressed because scales are fixed

Improved Version Using reorder_within()

bing_word_counts |>
  group_by(book, sentiment) |>
  slice_max(order_by = n, n = 5) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(word = reorder_within(word, n, book)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(word, n, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(sentiment ~ book, scales = "free") +
  scale_x_reordered() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(
    y = "Contribution to sentiment",
    x = NULL
  ) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(end = .9)

Improvements:

  • Each facet has its own ordering
  • Both axes are free, so scales adjust
  • Much clearer comparison

Sentence‑Level Sentiment Analysis

We now analyze Pride and Prejudice at the sentence level.

Prepare Sentence Tokens

tibble(text = prideprejudice) |>
  mutate(
    chapter = cumsum(str_detect(
      text,
      regex("^chapter [\\divxlc]", ignore_case = TRUE)
    )),
    text = str_replace(text, "(Chapter \\d+)", "\\1\\."),
    text = str_replace_all(text, "((Mr)|(Mrs)|(Dr))\\.", "\\1")
  ) |>
  unnest_tokens(sentence, text, token = "sentences") ->
PandP_sentences

What this does:

  • Adds chapter numbers
  • Ensures chapter headings end with a period
  • Removes periods after “Mr.”, “Mrs.”, “Dr.”
  • Tokenizes into sentences

Compute Sentence‑Level Sentiment

PandP_sentences |>
  mutate(sentence_number = row_number()) |>
  unnest_tokens(word, sentence) |>
  inner_join(get_sentiments("bing"), by = "word",
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  filter(chapter > 0) |>
  count(chapter, sentence_number, sentiment) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = sentiment, values_from = n,
    values_fill = list(n = 0)
  ) |>
  mutate(sentence_sent = positive - negative) |>
  mutate(sentence_sent = case_when(
    sentence_sent > 0 ~ 1,
    sentence_sent == 0 ~ 0,
    sentence_sent < 0 ~ -1
  )) |>
  group_by(chapter) |>
  summarize(
    chap_sent_per = sum(sentence_sent) / n(),
    .groups = "keep"
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(chapter, chap_sent_per)) +
  geom_line() +
  ggtitle("Sentence Sentiment Score per Chapter") +
  ylab("(Score/Total Sentences in a Chapter") +
  xlab("Chapter") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", alpha = .4, lty = 2) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(1, 61)) +
  geom_rug(sides = "b")

Interpretation:

  • Shows the emotional arc of the novel
  • Peaks = positive chapters
  • Valleys = negative chapters
  • Red line = neutral baseline

Chapter‑Level Negative Sentiment Across All Austen Books

We now find the most negative chapter in each book.

Prepare Negative Lexicon (Remove “miss”)

get_sentiments("bing") |>
  filter(sentiment == "negative") |>
  filter(word != "miss") ->
bingnegative

Count Words per Chapter

tidy_books |>
  group_by(book, chapter) |>
  summarize(words = n(), .groups = "drop") ->
wordcounts

Count Negative Words per Chapter

tidy_books |>
  semi_join(bingnegative, by = "word") |>
  group_by(book, chapter) |>
  summarize(negativewords = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  left_join(wordcounts, by = c("book", "chapter")) |>
  mutate(ratio = negativewords / words) |>
  filter(chapter != 0) |>
  ungroup() |>
  group_by(book) |>
  slice_max(order_by = ratio) |>
  ungroup()
# A tibble: 6 × 5
  book                chapter negativewords words  ratio
  <fct>                 <int>         <int> <int>  <dbl>
1 Sense & Sensibility      43           156  3405 0.0458
2 Pride & Prejudice        34           111  2104 0.0528
3 Mansfield Park           46           161  3685 0.0437
4 Emma                     16            81  1894 0.0428
5 Northanger Abbey         21           143  2982 0.0480
6 Persuasion                4            62  1807 0.0343

Interpretation:

This identifies the most negative chapter in each novel.

The results match the narrative:

  • Sense and Sensibility Ch. 43 — Marianne nearly dies
  • Pride and Prejudice Ch. 34 — Darcy’s disastrous first proposal
  • Mansfield Park Ch. 46 — Henry’s adultery scandal
  • Emma Ch. 16 — Emma’s self‑recrimination
  • Northanger Abbey Ch. 21 — Catherine’s Gothic fantasies
  • Persuasion Ch. 4 — Anne’s heartbreak flashback

Word Cloud Plots

Word clouds are one of the most recognizable text‑analysis visualizations.
They’re fun, fast, and visually appealing — but they’re also statistically weak.

Still, they’re worth understanding because:

  • They’re widely used in business and media
  • They’re easy to generate
  • They’re useful for exploratory analysis

Let’s walk through everything step‑by‑step.

Load {wordcloud}

library(wordcloud)
Loading required package: RColorBrewer

Basic Word Cloud (with stop words removed)

tidy_books |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  count(word) |>
  with(wordcloud(word, n, max.words = 30))

What this does:

  1. Removes stop words
  2. Counts remaining words
  3. Passes word and n to wordcloud()
  4. Displays the top 30 words by frequency

Word Cloud with Custom Stop Words (removing “miss”)

## Custom stop words - no miss
tidy_books |>
  anti_join(custom_stop_words, by = "word") |>
  count(word) |>
  with(wordcloud(word, n, max.words = 30))

Why this matters:

  • “miss” is extremely common in Austen
  • But it’s a title, not a negative verb
  • Removing it gives a more meaningful cloud

Limitations of Word Clouds

Word clouds are not good for:

  • Comparing frequencies
  • Showing precise values
  • Comparing across books or authors
  • Statistical analysis

They:

  • Lack a common axis
  • Emphasize visual impression over accuracy
  • Hide differences in scale

Better alternatives include:

  • Sorted bar charts
  • Faceted bar charts
  • Heatmaps
  • Scatterplots
  • Network plots

ChatterPlot

Alternative Visualization: Top 50 Words by Sentiment and Book

This uses {ggrepel} to avoid overlapping labels.

library(ggrepel)

tidy_books |>
  inner_join(bing_no_miss, by = "word",
             relationship = "many-to-many") |>
  count(book, word, sentiment, sort = TRUE) |>
  mutate(proportion = n / sum(n)) |>
  group_by(sentiment) |>
  slice_max(order_by = n, n = 50) |>
  ungroup() ->
tempp

tempp |>
  ggplot(aes(book, proportion, label = word)) +
  geom_text_repel(
    segment.alpha = 0,
    aes(
      color = sentiment, size = proportion
    ),
    max.overlaps = 50
  ) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(3, 6), guide = "none") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90)) +
  ggtitle("Top 50 Words by Sentiment in Each Book") +
  scale_color_viridis_d(end = .9)

Why this is better than a word cloud:

  • Words are placed meaningfully
  • Size encodes proportion
  • Color encodes sentiment
  • Books appear on the x‑axis
  • Labels repel each other

This is a true analytical visualization, not just decorative.

Heatmaps — A More Powerful Alternative

Heatmaps allow:

  • Direct comparison across books
  • A shared color scale
  • Clear patterns of usage
  • Easy identification of distinctive words

We’ll build two heatmaps:

  1. Global top shared words
  2. Top shared words within each book

Step 1 — Count non‑stop words by book

word_counts <- tidy_books |>
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") |>
  count(book, word)

Step 2 — Keep only words appearing in ≥ 3 books

shared_words <- word_counts |>
  group_by(word) |>
  summarize(n_books = n_distinct(book), .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(n_books >= 3)

This removes:

  • Rare words
  • Names
  • Book‑specific vocabulary

Step 3 — Select top shared words overall

top_words <- word_counts |>
  semi_join(shared_words, by = "word") |>
  group_by(word) |>
  summarize(total = sum(n), .groups = "drop") |>
  slice_max(order_by = total, n = 20) |>
  pull(word)

Step 4 — Heatmap of Global Top Words

word_counts |>
  filter(word %in% top_words) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = book, y = fct_reorder(word, n), fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(end = .9) +
  labs(
    title = "Top Shared Words Across All Jane Austen Books",
    subtitle = "Words selected based on overall frequency and appearing in multiple books",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    fill = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

Interpretation:

  • Shows which words dominate across the entire corpus
  • Uses a common color scale
  • Easy to compare across books

Step 5 — Top Shared Words Within Each Book

top_shared_words <- word_counts |>
  semi_join(shared_words, by = "word") |>
  group_by(book) |>
  slice_max(order_by = n, n = 20) |>
  ungroup()

Step 6 — Heatmap of Within‑Book Top Words

top_shared_words |>
  ggplot(aes(x = book, y = fct_reorder(word, n), fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(end = .9) +
  labs(
    title = "Top Words in Each Book (Shared Across at Least Three Books)",
    subtitle = "Words are selected within each book and restricted to those appearing in multiple books",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL,
    fill = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

How to Interpret the Two Heatmaps

Heatmap 1 — Global Top Words
  • Selects words based on overall frequency
  • Shows how those words vary across books
  • Good for corpus‑level comparison
Heatmap 2 — Within‑Book Top Words
  • Selects top words within each book
  • A word may appear in ≥3 books but only be top‑20 in some
  • Good for book‑specific patterns

Final Thoughts on Word Clouds vs Alternatives

Word clouds:

  • Fun
  • Quick
  • Good for presentations
  • Bad for analysis

Better tools:

  • Bar charts
  • Faceted comparisons
  • Heatmaps
  • Scatterplots
  • Network diagrams

These give real insight, not just visual flair.
